subject: ap-government
theme: Americans share a broad political culture rooted in liberty, equality, and free enterprise, but individuals diverge in political ideology based on socialization experiences, generational effects, and group identity — differences measured and tracked through public opinion polling.

core_concepts.title: Political Socialization Agents
core_concepts.detail: Political socialization is the lifelong process of acquiring political values and orientations; family is the most powerful agent, followed by school, peer groups, religion, and media — and major historical events can produce lasting generational effects on entire age cohorts.

core_concepts.title: Liberal, Conservative & Libertarian Ideology
core_concepts.detail: The liberal-conservative ideological spectrum captures disagreement about the role of government: liberals favor an active government role in reducing economic inequality and expanding civil rights; conservatives favor limited government, traditional values, and free-market solutions; libertarians oppose government intervention in both economic and personal domains.

core_concepts.title: Party Identification & Vote Choice
core_concepts.detail: Party identification — a voter's long-term psychological attachment to a political party — is the single strongest predictor of vote choice; it is formed early through socialization and is more durable than issue positions or candidate evaluations.

core_concepts.title: Polling Validity & Sampling Error
core_concepts.detail: Valid public opinion polling requires a random, representative sample; results are expressed with a margin of error (sampling error) reflecting the precision of sample-based estimation; question wording and question order can systematically bias responses.

core_concepts.title: Fiscal vs. Monetary Policy
core_concepts.detail: Fiscal policy (government taxation and spending) and monetary policy (Federal Reserve management of money supply and interest rates) represent two major axes of ideological disagreement — conservatives generally prefer fiscal restraint and low taxes; liberals favor government spending on social programs like Medicare and Medicaid.

core_concepts.title: Issue Salience & Wedge Issues
core_concepts.detail: Issue salience determines whether an opinion translates into political action: valence issues (e.g., economic growth) produce agreement on goals but disagreement on means; wedge issues (e.g., abortion, immigration) split electorates and are strategically deployed by campaigns to mobilize base voters.

exam_tip: The AP exam tests polling methodology critically — be prepared to explain why a biased sample, leading question wording, or poor question order can invalidate poll results, and know that sampling error is a statistical property of all sample-based surveys (not a mistake), while also connecting party identification and political socialization to vote choice and electoral outcomes.
